title: Vectorized Conditionals with ifelse()
description: Use ifelse() to apply conditions element-wise on vectors.

slides.title: The ifelse() Function
slides.content:
`if` works on a single value. For vectors, we use `ifelse()`, which is vectorized.

Syntax:
```r
ifelse(test, yes, no)
```
- `test` is a logical vector.
- `yes` is the value (or vector) returned when test is TRUE.
- `no` is the value (or vector) returned when test is FALSE.

The result is a vector of the same length as `test`, with elements chosen from `yes` or `no` accordingly.

quizData.question: What is the result of `ifelse(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE), 1, 2)`?
quizData.explanation: TRUE gives 1, FALSE gives 2, so result: 1 2 1.

slides.title: Nested ifelse()
slides.content:
You can nest `ifelse` calls to handle multiple conditions, but it can become hard to read. For more complex logic, consider using `dplyr::case_when()`.

```r
x <- c(10, 25, 30, 5)
ifelse(x < 15, "low", ifelse(x < 25, "medium", "high"))
```

slides.title: Nested ifelse()
slides.codeSnippet:
scores <- c(92, 67, 84, 55)
grades <- ifelse(scores >= 90, "A",
          ifelse(scores >= 80, "B",
          ifelse(scores >= 70, "C", "F")))
print(grades)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: `ifelse()` recycles `yes` and `no` if they are shorter than `test`. This can be useful but be careful with length mismatches.
